questions.text: At a system level, what is a thread defined as?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A set of resources capable of supporting execution of an instruction stream

choices.label: C
choices.text: A complete copy of the operating system

choices.label: D
choices.text: A block of data stored in DDR2 RAM

questions.explanation: According to the slides, at the system level, a thread is a set of resources capable of supporting execution of an instruction stream.

questions.text: In a shared memory programming model, what do threads share?
questions.explanation: In shared memory programs, several threads in the same process share the same memory (the address space).

questions.text: What is the term for the size of the pieces of code executed concurrently in a parallel program?
questions.explanation: Granularity of parallelism refers to the size of the pieces of code executed concurrently, ranging from fine-grain to coarse-grain.

questions.text: Which dependency exists if Statement \(S_2\) reads a value that was written by Statement \(S_1\)?
questions.explanation: \(S_2\) has a true dependence on \(S_1\) if \(S_2\) reads a value written by \(S_1\) (also known as Read-After-Write or RAW).
\(S_2\) writes to variable 'a' which was previously read by \(S_1\). This is an anti-dependence (Write-After-Read).

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding 'Embarrassingly Parallel' patterns?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The tasks are highly dependent on each other

choices.label: C
choices.text: The tasks can be executed with little to no interaction or synchronization

questions.explanation: Embarrassingly parallel tasks (like many simple loops) require little to no communication or dependency management between them.

questions.text: What happens if a programmer uses 'replication' to eliminate a dependency (e.g., calculating a value twice instead of passing it)?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It reduces observed speedup but can be useful

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is only possible in distributed memory

questions.explanation: Replication reduces the observed speedup and introduces overheads because of redundant work, but it can be useful to enable parallelism.

questions.text: In a loop-carried dependence, where does the dependence occur?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Outside of the loop structure entirely

choices.label: C
choices.text: Between statement instances in different iterations

questions.explanation: A loop-carried dependence is a dependence between a pair of statement instances in two different iterations of a loop nest.

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because there is a dependence within the statement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the dependence is loop-independent

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because it is an output dependence

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only if we use task parallelism

questions.explanation: The dependence is within the same iteration (a[i] is read and written in the same 'i'). There is no dependence between different iterations, so it is parallelizable.

questions.text: In the context of the Cell processor or modern cores with limited memory, what type of granularity is sometimes required?
questions.explanation: While conventional wisdom suggests larger granularity is better, hardware like IBM's Cell processor often needed fine-grained parallelism.

questions.text: Which of the following describes an Output Dependence?

choices.label: C
choices.text: \(S_2\) writes to a variable that was also written by \(S_1\)

choices.label: D
choices.text: \(S_2\) is controlled by the output of a branch in \(S_1\)

questions.explanation: Output dependence occurs when two statements write to the same memory location/variable (Write-After-Write).

questions.text: What is the primary goal of loop interchange (exchange)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To reduce the number of iterations

choices.label: B
choices.text: To move parallelizable loops to the outermost position

choices.label: D
choices.text: To convert data parallelism into task parallelism

questions.explanation: Loop interchange is used to move parallelizable loops to the outermost position, which often increases the granularity of parallelism and improves performance.
Iteration \(i=2\) reads \(a[1]\), which was written by iteration \(i=1\). This is a loop-carried true dependence, preventing parallel execution.

questions.text: What is meant by 'Execution Context' in the context of threads?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The physical location of the CPU

choices.label: B
choices.text: The state required to execute a thread (registers, stack pointer, etc.)

choices.label: C
choices.text: The total amount of memory available to the process

choices.label: D
choices.text: The sequence of messages sent in a distributed system

questions.explanation: Execution context refers to the state (like register values) that defines where a thread is in its instruction stream and its current data environment.

questions.text: In a distributed memory model, how do processes communicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: By reading and writing to a common address space

choices.label: B
choices.text: By sharing the same L2 cache

choices.label: C
choices.text: By sending messages to each other

questions.explanation: In distributed memory models, processes have distinct memories and interact by sending messages.

questions.text: If two statements \(S_1\) and \(S_2\) can be executed in any order without changing the result, they are:
questions.explanation: Statements are independent if their order of execution does not matter, meaning they can safely execute in parallel.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lack of information about the values in `index[]`

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is a confirmed output dependence

questions.explanation: The compiler cannot determine if there is a dependence without knowing the contents of `index[]`. If two indices are the same or refer to a previously written element, a dependence exists.

questions.text: What does the 'level of loop-carried dependence' indicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The total number of dependencies in the code

choices.label: B
choices.text: The nesting depth of the loop that carries the dependence

questions.explanation: The level corresponds to the depth of the loop that carries the dependence, determining which loops in a nest can or cannot be parallelized.

questions.text: Which of these is a 'hidden' output dependence?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Two `printf` statements writing to the same file stream

choices.label: D
choices.text: Using a local variable in a function

questions.explanation: I/O operations (like `printf`) have a hidden output dependence because they update the same output stream, and changing their order changes the file contents.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only the inner loop (j) can be parallelized

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only the outer loop (i) can be parallelized

questions.explanation: The inner loop (j) has a dependence (j depends on j-1). The outer loop (i) has no dependence across different values of 'i', so 'i' can be parallelized.

questions.text: What is the result of 'Chop up the data domain' pattern?
questions.explanation: Giving each thread a portion of the data domain to compute is called Data Parallelism.

questions.text: Which condition makes loop interchange ILLEGAL?

choices.label: B
choices.text: If it changes the order in which data objects are accessed such that the outcome changes

questions.explanation: Loop interchange is only legal if the relative order of data accesses that have a dependence is preserved.

questions.text: What is 'Coarse-grain' parallelism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Parallelism at the individual instruction level

choices.label: B
choices.text: Parallelism at the level of procedures or whole programs

questions.explanation: Coarse-grain parallelism refers to larger pieces of code, such as procedures or entire programs, being executed concurrently.

questions.text: How can you remove a dependence like `T = T + f(K)` in a loop to enable parallelism?

choices.label: B
choices.text: By replacing the variable `T` with an array to store intermediate values

questions.explanation: As shown in Example 15, replacing a scalar variable that creates a dependence with an array (expansion) can sometimes allow for parallel execution.

questions.text: What does 'Time-slicing' achieve in a single-core system?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The appearance of working simultaneously by switching between threads

questions.explanation: Time-slicing allows multiple threads to share a single core by giving each a small window of time, creating the illusion of concurrency.

questions.text: What is a 'Loop-independent' dependence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A dependence that exists even if the code is not in a loop

choices.label: B
choices.text: A dependence between two different iterations

choices.label: C
choices.text: A dependence that only occurs in nested loops

choices.label: D
choices.text: A dependence that cannot be removed

questions.explanation: Loop-independent dependences occur within the same iteration of a loop nest (or outside of loops) and do not themselves prevent loop parallelization.

choices.label: B
choices.text: By splitting it into two separate loops

questions.explanation: By splitting (distributing) the loop into two, the first loop (calculating all `a[i]`) becomes fully parallelizable. The second loop still has a dependence but the transformation helps structure the parallelism.

questions.text: Why must you check 'hidden' dependences in functions `f(x)` and `g(x)`?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because they might update the same global variable (side effects)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because they use too much memory

choices.label: D
choices.text: Functions cannot be executed in parallel

questions.explanation: Functions may have hidden dependences if they access or modify shared global state or variables not passed as local parameters.

questions.text: What does 'Synchronization' do in parallel software?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensures two threads do not access the same memory location simultaneously in an unsafe way

questions.explanation: Synchronization is used to coordinate interactions, making one thread wait for another or preventing race conditions.

questions.text: Is it possible to parallelize a loop with a dependence of the form `a[i] = f(a[i-10])`?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, but only if we use loop interchange

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, partial parallel execution or pipelining is possible

questions.explanation: While complete parallel execution is impossible, dependencies with a distance (like 10) allow for some groups of iterations to run in parallel or for pipelined execution.

questions.text: Which architectural component usually contains the smallest, fastest memory for a core?
questions.explanation: Registers are the smallest and fastest storage level inside the core.

questions.text: True or False: In a shared memory model, threads can have some data of their own.
questions.explanation: The slides state: 'Threads share the address space – But they might also have some data of their own' (e.g., private variables).

questions.text: What is a 'Light-weight process' often used as a synonym for?
questions.explanation: Threads were historically often referred to as light-weight processes.

questions.text: Which fundamental assumption is made about thread execution order?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Threads always execute in the order they were created

choices.label: B
choices.text: Threads execute independently with no control over relative order unless specified

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thread 1 always finishes before Thread 2 starts

choices.label: D
choices.text: Operating systems ensure threads never overlap

questions.explanation: The Fundamental Assumption is that threads execute independently on their assigned resources: no control over order of execution unless specified by the programmer.

questions.text: If statement order affects the outcome, the statements are said to be:
questions.explanation: Dependences prevent parallel execution because changing the order of dependent statements changes the result.

questions.text: What is 'Task Parallelism'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Applying the same operation to different data elements

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dividing the overall work into different functional pieces or tasks

questions.explanation: Task parallelism involves chopping up the work into pieces (tasks) and assigning those pieces to threads.

questions.text: Which is an example of an 'Irregular' pattern mentioned in the patterns of parallelism?
questions.explanation: The slides list 'Irregular mesh' as one of the patterns (Divide & Conquer, Recursive, Pipeline, etc.).

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because there are no dependences in the code at all

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because the dependence is loop-independent relative to the outer loop

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because the compiler automatically removes the dependence

questions.explanation: The dependence is between `a[i][j]` and `a[i][j-1]`. This means for a fixed `i`, iterations of `j` are dependent. However, different values of `i` do not depend on each other.

questions.text: What is 'Fine-grain' parallelism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Parallelism at the level of whole programs

choices.label: B
choices.text: Parallelism at the level of individual instructions or statements

choices.label: D
choices.text: Parallelism only used in shared memory

questions.explanation: Fine-grain parallelism refers to very small pieces of work, like individual instructions or statements, being executed concurrently.

questions.text: What is a 'Message-passing' program characteristic?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Threads sharing a single address space

choices.label: C
choices.text: No ability to run on multiple processors

questions.explanation: Message-passing programs consist of independent processes that communicate by sending messages, usually in a distributed memory environment.
Both statements write to variable 'a'. This is an output dependence (WAW).

questions.text: Why is the order of statements in a loop sometimes re-ordered before splitting it into two loops?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To increase the number of threads

choices.label: B
choices.text: To handle cases where a dependence source comes after the sink textually

choices.label: C
choices.text: To reduce the amount of memory used

questions.explanation: Re-ordering statements allows us to manage anti-dependences so that loop splitting (distribution) can be applied safely.

questions.text: Which of the following describes 'Divide & Conquer' in parallelism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A way of creating parallel code by breaking a problem into sub-problems

choices.label: B
choices.text: A method to eliminate all output dependences

questions.explanation: Divide & Conquer is a pattern where a problem is recursively divided into independent tasks that can be solved in parallel.

questions.text: Can two statements `a = 1;` and `b = 2;` execute in parallel?

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, they have a true dependence

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only if they are in a loop

questions.explanation: There are no shared variables between the two statements (no RAW, WAR, or WAW dependences), so they can execute in parallel.

questions.text: What is the primary constraint on achieving parallelism in software?
questions.explanation: Dependences (true, anti, and output) are the fundamental constraints that limit which parts of a program can execute concurrently.

questions.text: In a modern multicore processor, where might threads in the same core share resources?
questions.explanation: Threads running on the same core often share L1 and L2 caches, among other core-local resources.

questions.text: What term is used for the process of determining which loop iterations can run simultaneously?
questions.explanation: Identifying dependences (Dependency Analysis) is the key to finding which loops can be parallelized.

questions.text: Which of the following describes a 'Wavefront' pattern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: All iterations depend on the very first one

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dependences allow a 'diagonal' of tasks to execute in parallel

questions.explanation: In a wavefront, data dependences exist such that you can process elements in a 'wave' (e.g., diagonally across a matrix) where elements in the same wave are independent.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It makes the inner loop parallelizable

choices.label: C
choices.text: It moves the parallelizable loop `i` to the outside, increasing granularity

choices.label: D
choices.text: It decreases memory use by removing `a[i][j]`

questions.explanation: In the original, only the inner loop `i` is parallelizable. By swapping, `i` becomes the outer loop, allowing larger chunks of work (entire `j` loops) to be distributed, which is usually better for performance.

questions.text: What is the starting point for most parallelization efforts?
questions.explanation: As noted in the slides, the starting point for parallel software is often a sequential program.
